name: page-rastered-training-corrected-20260721
supersedes: page-rastered-training-20260721.json (that run was VOID - three data-integrity bugs)
question: With verified-clean training data, does page-rastered (row-sliced) training beat the psm-7 synthetic model and stock Tesseract on byte-restore for base32g?
verdict: NO. The corrected page-rastered model FAILS the byte-restore gate clean and blurred, while the psm-7 synthetic control and stock Tesseract both restore byte-identical. The hypothesis is refuted on its merits this time: the data defects that voided the first attempt were fixed and verified, and the approach still loses.

environment.font: courier (Nimbus Mono PS on the VM)

data_integrity_fixes_applied_before_this.1_banner_off_by_one: frame_lines() drops the display-only #!glyphive banner from ground truth, but slice_rows() still cut from the banner's physical position, so crop i was paired with line i+1. Measured before fix: 266/300 sampled pairs mismatched. Fixed with a row_offset.
data_integrity_fixes_applied_before_this.2_geometric_row_overcount: rows-per-page was estimated as (height - margin) // leading = 78 on pages that actually printed 58, so tail crops fell past the last printed line and pairing drifted from that point on. Fixed by reading the authoritative per-page printed-row count from the PDF text layer.
data_integrity_fixes_applied_before_this.3_ocr_derived_box_labels: the lstmbox step labels box files with what STOCK OCR READ, not with the ground truth, injecting characters outside the codec alphabet (S/Q/W/O/section-sign/pipe) into the .lstmf transcriptions - i.e. training the model to reproduce stock's mistakes. Fixed by emitting a WordStr line box carrying the authoritative transcription.
data_integrity_fixes_applied_before_this.verification: encode failures 7 -> 0; skip ratio 35.000% -> 0.000% sustained across the entire run; pair check sampled 80 rows with 3 near-misses attributable to OCR noise, none to mispairing.

training.method: page-rastered: create --format pdf -> rasterize 300 DPI -> slice rows by authoritative printed-row count -> WordStr box from ground truth -> tesseract --psm 6 lstm.train
training.note: training plateaued and selected its best checkpoint at 5400 rather than running to 10000

gate.definition: byte-identical restore: create -> rasterize 300 DPI -> OCR per page (whitelist = codec spec.alphabet + delimiter, --psm 6) -> extract -> byte-diff the tree
gate.corpus: 2 real markdown guides, 21658 bytes, 10 pages, 678 OCR lines

results.detail: cannot recover RS parameters: data/parity line counts are inconsistent (missing or spurious lines)
line L00007 failed CRC and exceeds RS correction budget
line L00001 failed CRC and exceeds RS correction budget
line L00001 failed CRC and exceeds RS correction budget

analysis.failure_mode: The corrected model's clean-image failure is a LINE-COUNT error (data/parity line counts inconsistent), not a substitution error - it still merges or drops printed rows. That is the same lethal mechanism that killed basemaxg, base64g and PaddleOCR: this format tolerates a misread character (CRC/RS repair it in place) far better than a change in line length or line count, which desynchronizes the frame parse.
analysis.cer_finally_agrees_with_restore: The plan's stated acceptance criterion was a model whose reported eval error and byte-restore outcome agree in DIRECTION. That is now satisfied - just not favourably. This model reports 2.081% BCER (honest, measured against correctly-paired ground truth) and fails restore, whereas the earlier ~0% CER figures were measured against corrupted labels and were meaningless. An honest metric and an honest outcome now point the same way.
analysis.why_synthetic_wins: The psm-7 synthetic model trains on isolated, generously-spaced single lines, which never teaches row-merging. Page-rastered crops carry inter-line context, and the model learns to merge or split rows - exactly the behaviour the format cannot absorb.

recommendation: Do not ship any base32g model. Stock Tesseract + the codec whitelist restores base32g byte-identical, clean and blurred, with no model at all - it is the baseline none of the three trained approaches beat. Page-level (row-sliced) training is retired for this format; option (b) from the original plan, whole-page --psm 6 training without slicing, is untried but has no supporting evidence and would face the same line-merging risk.
